content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

May 4, 1990

Brian W. Maas
Pillsbury, Madison & Sutro
P.O. Box 7880
San Francisco, CA  94120

Re: Your Request for Informal
    Assistance
    Our File No. I-90-122

Dear Mr. Maas:

You have requested informal advice concerning the lobbying
provisions of the Political Reform Act.¹

QUESTION

Is an employee who spends less than 50 percent of his or her
time attempting to influence legislative or administrative action
exempt from the definition of a lobbyist as an employee who has
been engaged "primarily to perform services other than influencing
legislative or administrative action?"

CONCLUSION

Without specific facts concerning the duties, salary received
for lobbying activities in a calendar month, and amount of time
spent by the employee on influencing or attempting to influence
legislative or administrative action (lobbying activities), a
clear conclusion cannot be made. An employee who spends less than
50 percent of his or her time on lobbying activities may still
qualify as a lobbyist.


1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

Brian W. Maas
Page Two

ANALYSIS

Section 82039 defines a "lobbyist" as:

...any individual who is employed or contracts for
economic consideration .... to communicate directly
or through his or her agents with any elective state
official, agency official or legislative official
for the purpose of influencing legislative or administrative action, if a substantial or regular portion
of the activities for which he or she receives consideration is for the purpose of influencing legislative or administrative action.

Regulation 18239 establishes two tests for determining when
an individual's activities in connection with influencing
legislative or administrative action are "substantial or regular."
The tests are commonly referred to as the "contacts" test and the
"compensation" test. The "contacts" test provides that a lobbyist
is an individual who:

...receives or becomes entitled to receive any amount of
compensation for engaging in direct communication, other than
administrative testimony, with qualifying officials for the
purpose of influencing legislative or administrative action
on at least 25 separate occasions in any two consecutive
calendar months.

Regulation 18239(c).

The "compensation" test provides that an individual who
engages in any amount of direct communication may also qualify as
a lobbyist if he or she:

...receives or becomes entitled to receive at least $2,000 in
compensation in any calendar month for influencing
legislative or administrative action.

Regulation 18239(b).

However, Regulation 18239(b) excludes from the "compensation" test
a "full time employee engaged primarily to perform services other
than influencing legislative or administrative action, or for
administrative testimony."

There is nothing in the Act, regulations, or past advice that
provides a percentage for determining when an employee has been
engaged "primarily" to perform services other than lobbying. An
employee who regularly spends 50 percent or more of his or her
time on lobbying activities would certainly not be excluded from

Brian W. Maas
Page Three

the definition of "lobbyist" by the exception in Regulation
18239(b). On the other hand, Commission regulations specifically
exclude from disclosure compensation paid by a lobbyist employer
to employees other than lobbyists who spend less than 10 percent
of their compensated time on lobbying activities. Therefore, it
could be assumed that an individual who does not meet the
"contacts" test and who spends less than 10 percent of his or her
compensated time on lobbying activities would be covered by the
exception.

However, whether a particular individual who spends more than
10 percent of his or her time engaged in lobbying activities is
considered to be engaged "primarily" to perform services other
than lobbying will depend on the specific facts involved. One
factor to consider in making such a determination would be whether
the individual regularly spends more than 10 percent of his or her
time each month on lobbying activities, including direct
communication with qualifying officials.

If you have any additional questions, please contact me at
(916) 322-5662.

Sincerely,

Kathryn E. Donovan
General Counsel

By: [illegible]
Kevin S. Braaten-Moen
Political Reform Consultant

Enclosure
